CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the intro, method (vector-space construction, PCA axis, reference vectors), experiments and conclusion follow a logical flow. Notation appears consistent in the summary and figures/tables are described as effective, but important presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters (and code) are not reported, which reduce reproducibility. The writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, though a brief explicit statement of experimental settings and metrics would make the presentation fully unambiguous. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and clearly (novel Word2Vec-based sentiment orientation, robustness to sparsity, full-vocabulary scoring). Notation appears consistent from the summary but some presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters are not reported, which reduces reproducibility and clarity of the experimental section. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good, though the lack of detail about figures/tables and explicit experimental reporting are minor issues preventing a top score.

The writing quality is good and related work coverage is thorough, though a brief explicit statement of experimental settings and metrics would make the presentation fully unambiguous.

Notation appears consistent from the summary but some presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified and hyperparameters are not reported, which reduces reproducibility and clarity of the experimental section. Related work coverage is thorough and writing quality is described as good, though the lack of detail about figures/tables and explicit experimental reporting are minor issues preventing a top score.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles the important problem of measuring word-level sentiment orientation and offers a practical unsupervised alternative to Turney’s PMI-IR that reportedly outperforms it on much smaller datasets and can produce orientation values for the entire vocabulary (not just matched phrases). From a methodological-reproducibility perspective, the approach—building Word2Vec vectors, using PCA to find a sentiment axis, and deriving reference vectors—is a useful, interpretable recipe that practitioners could adopt. However, the contribution appears incremental rather than transformative: it primarily improves an existing unsupervised baseline and lacks details (e.g., metrics, hyperparameters, code) in the summary that would be needed to judge generality and drive broad adoption or new theoretical directions. Overall, this yields moderate impact — likely useful and cited but not field-changing. | B(3): The paper addresses a relevant problem—unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation—and offers a practical solution that outperforms the classic PMI-IR method while using much smaller corpora (IMDB experiments) and producing orientations for all vocabulary items. As an embedding-based, PCA-driven technique built on Word2Vec, it is a useful incremental advance that is likely to be adopted and extended for low-resource or corpus-limited settings and for integrating with downstream sentiment tasks. However, the contribution is evolutionary rather than foundational: it leverages existing representation learning tools without proposing a new paradigm, so its influence is moderate rather than transformative.

However, the contribution appears incremental rather than transformative: it primarily improves an existing unsupervised baseline and lacks details (e.g., metrics, hyperparameters, code) in the summary that would be needed to judge generality and drive broad adoption or new theoretical directions. Overall, this yields moderate impact — likely useful and cited but not field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a relevant problem—unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation—and offers a practical solution that outperforms the classic PMI-IR method while using much smaller corpora (IMDB experiments) and producing orientations for all vocabulary items. As an embedding-based, PCA-driven technique built on Word2Vec, it is a useful incremental advance that is likely to be adopted and extended for low-resource or corpus-limited settings and for integrating with downstream sentiment tasks. However, the contribution is evolutionary rather than foundational: it leverages existing representation learning tools without proposing a new paradigm, so its influence is moderate rather than transformative.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper applies established distributional semantic representations (Word2Vec) and a simple PCA-derived sentiment axis to the sentiment orientation task, which is a reasonable but incremental methodological shift from PMI-IR rather than a wholly new paradigm. The main contribution is a practical pipeline that leverages vector-space geometry to produce dense sentiment scores for all vocabulary items and shows robustness to data sparsity; however, these are straightforward combinations of known techniques (word embeddings, PCA, vector averaging) rather than fundamentally novel algorithms. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from Turney (2002) and demonstrate advantages on smaller corpora, so the work would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts as a major conceptual advance. | B(4): The paper applies distributional word embeddings and PCA to induce a sentiment axis and builds positive/negative reference vectors—an approach that is a clear methodological departure from PMI-IR and addresses its sparsity/coverage limitations. The formulation is not a new problem but presents a notable, non-trivial use of vector-space methods to measure word-level sentiment orientation and to label all vocabulary items. Given the reported empirical advantages on IMDB and clear differentiation from Turney (2002), the contribution would be useful and informative to researchers familiar with prior unsupervised sentiment methods.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies established distributional semantic representations (Word2Vec) and a simple PCA-derived sentiment axis to the sentiment orientation task, which is a reasonable but incremental methodological shift from PMI-IR rather than a wholly new paradigm. The main contribution is a practical pipeline that leverages vector-space geometry to produce dense sentiment scores for all vocabulary items and shows robustness to data sparsity; however, these are straightforward combinations of known techniques (word embeddings, PCA, vector averaging) rather than fundamentally novel algorithms. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from Turney (2002) and demonstrate advantages on smaller corpora, so the work would inform practitioners but is unlikely to surprise experts as a major conceptual advance.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper demonstrates a plausible new Word2Vec+PCA pipeline and compares it to Turney’s PMI-IR on IMDB and SST, which provides a reasonable proof-of-concept, but key experimental details are missing (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and random seeds not reported, and code not provided) limiting reproducibility. There are no reported ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of PCA dimensions, seed word choice, embedding hyperparameters, corpus size scaling) to attribute gains to specific design choices. Related work appears well covered and the PMI-IR limitation regarding sparsity is discussed, but failure cases and statistical rigor (multiple runs, confidence intervals) are not addressed. Overall the experiments are adequate to suggest promise but lack the thorough controls and reporting needed for strong empirical support. | B(3): The experiments provide a convincing proof-of-concept (comparison to Turney’s PMI-IR on IMDB and use of SST) but lack several analyses needed to fully support the claims: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters and code are not reported, and there are no ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., effect of PCA dimensionality, seed choice, corpus size). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper does acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitation, but failure modes and broader limitations (generalization beyond IMDB, multilinguality, downstream utility) are not explored. Overall, the empirical evidence is adequate to indicate promise but incomplete for strong claims of robustness and reproducibility.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper demonstrates a plausible new Word2Vec+PCA pipeline and compares it to Turney’s PMI-IR on IMDB and SST, which provides a reasonable proof-of-concept, but key experimental details are missing (evaluation metrics not specified, hyperparameters and random seeds not reported, and code not provided) limiting reproducibility. There are no reported ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of PCA dimensions, seed word choice, embedding hyperparameters, corpus size scaling) to attribute gains to specific design choices. Related work appears well covered and the PMI-IR limitation regarding sparsity is discussed, but failure cases and statistical rigor (multiple runs, confidence intervals) are not addressed. Overall the experiments are adequate to suggest promise but lack the thorough controls and reporting needed for strong empirical support.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments provide a convincing proof-of-concept (comparison to Turney’s PMI-IR on IMDB and use of SST) but lack several analyses needed to fully support the claims: evaluation metrics are not specified, hyperparameters and code are not reported, and there are no ablation or sensitivity studies (e.g., effect of PCA dimensionality, seed choice, corpus size). Related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper does acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitation, but failure modes and broader limitations (generalization beyond IMDB, multilinguality, downstream utility) are not explored. Overall, the empirical evidence is adequate to indicate promise but incomplete for strong claims of robustness and reproducibility.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares primarily to Turney’s PMI-IR (2002) and demonstrates advantages on smaller corpora, but it omits more recent and relevant distributional and embedding-based sentiment methods (e.g., modern unsupervised/lexicon induction approaches or supervised neural baselines). Comparisons appear partially fair (same IMDB data) and the authors acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and reduced test set, but they do not report evaluation metrics or multiple baselines to contextualize gains. The methodological differences are described, and related work coverage is said to be thorough, but the experimental comparison is incomplete for current standards and may not establish the claimed superiority robustly. | B(3): The paper compares its method primarily against the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) and explains why PMI-IR suffers from data sparsity on the smaller IMDB corpus, which is appropriate historical context. However, it omits more recent unsupervised and representation-based baselines (e.g., other embedding-based or lexicon induction methods) and appears to run PMI-IR under conditions that reduce its test set (7,646 vs 9,613 reviews), raising fairness concerns. The authors do discuss PMI-IR’s shortcomings, but differences are not situated against a broader set of competing modern approaches, limiting the conclusiveness of the empirical claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares primarily to Turney’s PMI-IR (2002) and demonstrates advantages on smaller corpora, but it omits more recent and relevant distributional and embedding-based sentiment methods (e.g., modern unsupervised/lexicon induction approaches or supervised neural baselines). Comparisons appear partially fair (same IMDB data) and the authors acknowledge PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and reduced test set, but they do not report evaluation metrics or multiple baselines to contextualize gains. The methodological differences are described, and related work coverage is said to be thorough, but the experimental comparison is incomplete for current standards and may not establish the claimed superiority robustly.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its method primarily against the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) and explains why PMI-IR suffers from data sparsity on the smaller IMDB corpus, which is appropriate historical context. However, it omits more recent unsupervised and representation-based baselines (e.g., other embedding-based or lexicon induction methods) and appears to run PMI-IR under conditions that reduce its test set (7,646 vs 9,613 reviews), raising fairness concerns. The authors do discuss PMI-IR’s shortcomings, but differences are not situated against a broader set of competing modern approaches, limiting the conclusiveness of the empirical claims.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear claims and presents a plausible Word2Vec+PCA pipeline with experiments on IMDB and SST showing improvement over PMI-IR, but important experimental details are missing that prevent full technical validation. Mathematical steps are straightforward (PCA and cosine differences) and appear correct, but hyperparameters, preprocessing, train/val/test splits, evaluation metrics and whether results are averaged over multiple runs or statistically tested are not reported. The experimental comparison is weakly controlled—only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, no ablations are provided to attribute gains to specific design choices (e.g., seed selection, dimensionality, PCA choices), and no code is provided—so claims about robustness to sparsity and vocabulary-wide applicability are insufficiently supported. Overall the method is promising and not obviously flawed, but the empirical evidence and methodological reporting are incomplete, so I rate it a 3. | B(3): The paper's central empirical claim—that a Word2Vec+PCA based unsupervised method outperforms PMI-IR on IMDB and is robust to data sparsity—is plausible and supported by experiments comparing to Turney (2002). However, the summary indicates missing experimental details (no evaluation metrics or statistical tests reported), limited baselines (only PMI-IR) and absent hyperparameter reporting, which weaken the strength of the evidence. There are also methodological choices that introduce potential unsupported assumptions (e.g., labeling the PCA sentiment axis using a single seed word ‘‘excellent’’) that are not theoretically justified. Overall the approach seems promising but the technical validation is incomplete, so the paper is not fully convincing on correctness.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents a plausible Word2Vec+PCA pipeline with experiments on IMDB and SST showing improvement over PMI-IR, but important experimental details are missing that prevent full technical validation. Mathematical steps are straightforward (PCA and cosine differences) and appear correct, but hyperparameters, preprocessing, train/val/test splits, evaluation metrics and whether results are averaged over multiple runs or statistically tested are not reported. The experimental comparison is weakly controlled—only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, no ablations are provided to attribute gains to specific design choices (e.g., seed selection, dimensionality, PCA choices), and no code is provided—so claims about robustness to sparsity and vocabulary-wide applicability are insufficiently supported. Overall the method is promising and not obviously flawed, but the empirical evidence and methodological reporting are incomplete, so I rate it a 3.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's central empirical claim—that a Word2Vec+PCA based unsupervised method outperforms PMI-IR on IMDB and is robust to data sparsity—is plausible and supported by experiments comparing to Turney (2002). However, the summary indicates missing experimental details (no evaluation metrics or statistical tests reported), limited baselines (only PMI-IR) and absent hyperparameter reporting, which weaken the strength of the evidence. There are also methodological choices that introduce potential unsupported assumptions (e.g., labeling the PCA sentiment axis using a single seed word ‘‘excellent’’) that are not theoretically justified. Overall the approach seems promising but the technical validation is incomplete, so the paper is not fully convincing on correctness.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses a core NLP/ML topic (unsupervised distributional sentiment orientation) that is plainly within scope for top conferences and targets the right audience (researchers in distributional semantics and sentiment analysis). The problem formulation—computing word-level sentiment orientation via word vectors and PCA—is appropriate for the venue. However, several expected experimental details and evaluation standards appear missing in the summary (no reported evaluation metrics, hyperparameters, code availability, or multiple-run/statistical analysis), which weakens reproducibility and the methodological standards expected at top venues. Overall it is a good fit but would benefit from fuller experimental rigor to reach a perfect fit. | B(4): This paper addresses a core NLP problem — unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation using modern distributional vectors — and evaluates on standard corpora (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank), so the topic and audience are well within a top-tier NLP/ML venue. However, the empirical framing shows some gaps for a flagship conference: comparisons are limited (only PMI-IR), evaluation metrics and hyperparameters are not reported, and code release is unclear, which raises reproducibility and benchmarking concerns that warrant revision rather than outright rejection.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem — unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation using modern distributional vectors — and evaluates on standard corpora (IMDB, Stanford Sentiment Treebank), so the topic and audience are well within a top-tier NLP/ML venue. However, the empirical framing shows some gaps for a flagship conference: comparisons are limited (only PMI-IR), evaluation metrics and hyperparameters are not reported, and code release is unclear, which raises reproducibility and benchmarking concerns that warrant revision rather than outright rejection.

main_claims: A novel distributional semantic (Word2Vec-based) approach to measure word-level sentiment orientation is introduced.
The proposed unsupervised methodology outperforms the previous unsupervised PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) approach while using much smaller datasets.
The method is robust to data sparsity and can compute sentiment orientation values for all words in the vocabulary (unlike Turney’s phrase-oriented approach).
_summary.methodology: Construct a high-dimensional vector space from the IMDB dataset, select adjective/adverb point words, use PCA (2 dimensions) to find a sentiment principal axis, build positive/negative reference vectors by averaging vectors on each side of the axis (labelled using the seed word "excellent"), and define a word's sentiment orientation as the difference between its cosine distances to the positive and negative reference vectors; classify reviews by the mean orientation across words/phrases.
limitations: PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) suffers from data sparsity when the corpus is not large enough to compute reliable PMI.
PMI-IR is only applicable to reviews containing at least one of the defined patterns, reducing the test set (e.g., from 9,613 to 7,646 reviews in their experiment).

reviewer_a.name: Methodological Reproducibility Analyst
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years applying and evaluating distributional semantic models and classification pipelines in industry and academia, focusing on experimental rigor and reproducible engineering.
traits: checks for complete experimental details (train/val/test splits, hyperparameters, preprocessing, random seeds) to enable reproducibility
requires thorough baselines and ablation studies to attribute gains to specific design choices
evaluates statistical rigor (e.g., multiple runs, significance testing, confidence intervals)
inspects evaluation methodology for leakage, dataset size effects, and correct metric usage
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Emphasizes precise, actionable feedback on experiments, implementation details, and analyses needed to validate empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Sentiment Analysis & NLP Impact Specialist
reviewer_b.background: Senior academic researcher in NLP with a decade of publications on sentiment analysis, lexical semantics, and evaluation of unsupervised sentiment induction methods; tracks the evolution of representation learning and its impact on downstream tasks.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior work and whether the contribution fills an important gap in the literature
evaluates broader applicability and practical impact (e.g., generalization beyond IMDB, multilinguality, downstream usefulness)
notices conceptual clarity and alignment between claims and empirical evidence, including limits of the approach
looks for connections to existing representation learning methods and whether theoretical or empirical insights generalize
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on situating the work within the field, judging the significance of its contributions, and identifying what follow-up research or clarifications are needed for adoption.
